sourceMovie.title: Grease
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Grease (1978) as a romance theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The romance and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: 10 Things I Hate About You
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Grease clicked for you, 10 Things I Hate About You (1999) is a strong next stop: both trade in romance and comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Pitch Perfect
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Grease clicked for you, Pitch Perfect (2012) is a strong next stop: both trade in romance and comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Grease?
faq.answer: Try 10 Things I Hate About You, Pitch Perfect, High School Musical—each shares DNA with Grease while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Grease?
faq.answer: Grease (1978) is best described as Romance, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Grease part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Grease Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
